topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights hummed with an irritating, relentless buzz above the cluttered workbench. Elias dragged a hand across his forehead, the motion slow and heavy. He stared at the schematic spread across the table, the lines blurring into meaningless noise. He’d spent the last seventy-two hours wrestling with this particular algorithm, feeling the familiar tightening in his chest that accompanied prolonged mental friction. Then, he noticed the faint, almost identical smudge of dried coffee on the corner of the printout. A slow, tight sigh escaped him, a sound more like air escaping a punctured tire. He looked up, scanning the room, and his gaze snagged on a figure across the aisle—a person hunched over an identical setup, their posture mirroring his own strained concentration. Elias felt a sudden, sharp deflation, as if the air had been sucked from his lungs.

[story 2]

"This is ridiculous," muttered Clara, tapping her pen against the monitor with unnecessary force. Her jaw was set so tightly she could feel the muscles ache. The data stream refused to cooperate, looping back on itself with infuriating consistency. She leaned back in her chair, the cheap plastic groaning under her weight, and rubbed her temples with both palms. It was the sheer, unyielding sameness of the debugging process that was grating on her nerves. Suddenly, her peripheral vision caught movement. Across the narrow workspace, another individual was staring at the exact same lines of code, a faint frown etched onto their face, mirroring her own deep-seated frustration. A low, almost inaudible sound of exasperation escaped her lips, a sound that promised utter defeat.

[story 3]

Marcus picked at a loose thread on his sleeve, his movements jerky and restless. The project—the intricate clockwork mechanism he was trying to perfect—was mocking him. Every adjustment seemed to yield the exact same, frustratingly flawed result. He let out a slow, audible exhale, the sound heavy with dissatisfaction. He shifted his weight, the cheap metal stool scraping against the concrete floor. As he straightened up to retrieve a tool, he saw it: another person, equally absorbed in the identical assembly, their brow furrowed in a mirroring expression of deep annoyance. Marcus stopped dead, his shoulders slumping slightly. A profound sense of weary resignation washed over him, making him want to simply dismantle everything and walk away.
